title: TypeScript
description: Everything you need to learn about TypeScript in 2025

topics.name: Any
topics.description: TypeScript has a special type, `any`, that you can use whenever you don’t want a particular value to cause typechecking errors.

topics.name: As Any
topics.description: `any` is a special type in TypeScript that represents a value of any type. When a value is declared with the any type, the compiler will not perform any type checks or type inference on that value.

topics.name: Compiler Options
topics.description: Compiler options in TypeScript provide fine-grained control over how the TypeScript compiler (`tsc`) transforms `.ts` files into JavaScript. These options are typically configured within a `tsconfig.json` file or passed directly via the command line.

topics.name: Generics

topics.name: Inheritance Vs Polymorphism

topics.name: Installation And Configuration
topics.description: The installation process of TypeScript generally includes installing the TypeScript compiler, which translates TypeScript code into JavaScript, and configuring your project with a `tsconfig.json` file to manage compiler options and project settings.

topics.name: Interface Declaration
topics.description: An interface declaration introduces a new named type into the TypeScript type system. It specifies a contract that an object (or other type) can adhere to, defining the names and types of properties and methods that implementing objects must possess.

topics.name: Interface

topics.name: Literal Types

topics.name: Never

topics.name: Non Null Assertion

topics.name: Nonnullable

topics.name: Null
topics.description: The `null` type represents the intentional absence of a value. It's a primitive data type that has only one possible value: `null`. It signifies that a variable or property has no value assigned to it, or that a value has been explicitly removed.

topics.name: Number
topics.description: The `number` type in TypeScript represents numeric values. This includes integers (whole numbers like 1, 42, -10) and floating-point numbers (numbers with decimal points like 3.14, -0.5).  TypeScript uses double-precision 64-bit floating point format

topics.name: Type Compatibility
topics.description: TypeScript uses structural typing to determine type compatibility. This means that two types are considered compatible if they have the same structure, regardless of their names.
